title: Integer Quantum Hall Response Equals Band And Many-Body Chern Number
summary: In a gapped integer quantum Hall system, the integer Hall-response level k equals both the source-grade TKNN band Chern number k' and the many-body twist-angle Chern number \widehat{k}'.
assumptions: The low-energy response is captured by an integer-level Chern-Simons term.
The many-body ground state remains nondegenerate under adiabatic twists.
regime: Integer quantum Hall systems with a stable gap.
scope: Canonical theorem family joining Witten Lecture Two, the TKNN band invariant, and the many-body twist-angle formulation.

source_anchors.notes: Original microscopic Hall-conductance invariant, now fused locally through the traced-curvature identity and the occupied-projector formula.
Neighboring lattice realization and parity-anomaly interpretation of the same Hall-response family through the valley-mass Chern-number criterion.

representation: Canonical theorem family joining response theory, projector-level band topology, and many-body adiabatic transport.
motivation: This note exists because Lecture Two has a flagship conclusion: the same integer controls macroscopic Hall response, microscopic band topology, and many-body adiabatic transport. Without writing that equality as one explicit theorem, the branch remains a collection of disconnected formulas.
prerequisite_story: Know the integer quantization of the Chern-Simons level, the derivation of the Hall current from the Chern-Simons response, the TKNN band invariant, and the twist-angle many-body Berry-curvature construction.
local_context_story: In Witten Lecture Two, this theorem is the culmination of the quantized Hall-response discussion. The macroscopic response coefficient k, the filled-band Chern number k', and the many-body twist-angle invariant khat' are shown to be the same integer.
forward_links_story: This theorem is reused in Lecture Three when the bulk integer Hall response is matched with the boundary chiral anomaly and the domain-wall edge mode.
canonical_questions: What are the three different integers that this theorem identifies?
Which sublemmas connect the response-theory, band-theory, and many-body formulations?
Why is the Haldane model a neighboring realization of the theorem rather than part of the theorem statement itself?
scope_boundaries: The theorem assumes a gapped integer Hall phase with nondegenerate adiabatic twist transport.
The theorem identifies the integer invariants of the same phase; it does not claim every nearby model has already been reduced to this common form.

math_expressions.notes: Equivalent projector formula for the TKNN invariant.
Many-body twist-angle Chern number equals the Hall-response level.
Canonical equality of the three integer invariants.

faithfulness_strategy: Keep the target theorem equal to the shared integer relation k = k' = khat', and treat the Haldane branch only as a neighboring realization rather than as an extra conjunct inside the theorem.
faithfulness_notes: The KB expands the proof spine into obligations and fragments but does not alter the source theorem statement.
comparator_risks: Do not weaken the theorem into only a band statement or only a many-body statement; all three integers must remain present.
attribution_requirements: Retain attribution to Witten for the theorem statement and to TKNN for the projector-level band invariant used in the proof route.
admissible_assumptions: Work in a gapped two-dimensional U(1)-conserving Hall phase with a nondegenerate ground state under adiabatic twists.
Treat the response, band, and many-body constructions as three presentations of the same phase rather than as independent systems.
formalization_blockers: The common Lean-side wrapper relating response theory, Bloch bundles, and many-body twist bundles is still missing.
The Haldane-model realization and the thermodynamic-limit bridge are still maintained as neighboring explanatory units rather than as local formal sublemmas.
retrieval_hints: Use when a query asks for the flagship theorem behind Lecture Two rather than only one side of the equivalence.
